Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
China Internet — E-commerce & Cloud/AI
2026-07-03
Initiation of Coverage

Alibaba Group Holding

HKEX (secondary NYSE: BABA): 9988.HK
InitiationChina Internet — E-commerce & CloudMega-cap · US$230bn

Executive Summary

Alibaba is priced as a structurally impaired ex-growth platform at the exact moment its operating story inflects: FY2026 (ended March 2026) carried a deliberate, management-chosen profit trough — adjusted EBITDA −44%, free cash flow −RMB47bn — to fund the quick-commerce land war and China's largest AI/cloud build-out. The market has capitalized that trough: at HK$94.10 the shares trade at the bottom of their 52-week range, a ~16% implied discount rate, and effectively zero value for a RMB974bn investment portfolio. The DCF values the shares at HK$224.63 base (HK$116–285 bear–bull); value realization runs through three observable mechanisms — quick-commerce subsidy normalization from FY2028, cloud/AI compounding at 30%+ with improving gross margin, and buyback resumption once FCF inflects.

Financial Snapshot↑

Adjusted EBITDA (company-reported) 113,483 (11.1%) 167,104 (14.7%)
Non-GAAP net income 60,658 —
Diluted EPS (RMB) 5.50 4.71

Net debt net cash 26,844 (BS basis); 260,828 incl. liquid treasury

NTM figures are model base case (Alibaba publishes no numeric guidance; consensus NTM EPS HK$9.23 per Yahoo implies 10.2x NTM P/E).

Investment Thesis↑

  • The core franchise is intact: CMR grew +7% like-for-like in FY2026 with a 19.4% segment EBITA margin in China e-commerce despite absorbing the entire quick-commerce investment — the cash engine is not broken, it is redeployed.
  • Cloud is compounding at 34–40% with AI products at triple-digit growth for 11 straight quarters and a 37% share of a market growing 26% — a second profit engine the core comps (PDD, JD, Meituan) do not own.
  • The FY2026 loss pools are discretionary: ~RMB86bn of incremental EBITA drag came from quick-commerce subsidies and Qwen-app user acquisition; three rational oligopolists and explicit regulator pressure on "involution" point to normalization, not a permanent war.
  • The balance sheet carries the transition: RMB520.8bn of liquid assets vs RMB260.0bn debt means the negative-FCF phase is internally funded, with no dilution or refinancing risk.
  • Valuation asymmetry is extreme: every cell of the WACC×g stress grid (HK$165–380) sits above the price; the bear case (HK$116) assumes the war persists and cloud halves its growth — and still exceeds HK$94.

Key Strengths↑

  • Two-engine model: China's #1 e-commerce monetization stack plus its #1 cloud/AI platform — full-stack from proprietary inference silicon (T-Head Zhenwu) to consumer application (Qwen app).
  • Merchant/brand relationships and 88VIP (62m+ high-spend members, double-digit growth) anchor the highest-quality ad dollars in Chinese retail.
  • Net-cash fortress balance sheet plus a RMB974bn investment portfolio (incl. ~33% of Ant Group) valued near zero by the market.
  • Supply-constrained cloud pricing: industry hardware costs have doubled in two years, handing incumbent capacity owners pricing power management already sees in gross margin.

Key Risks↑

  • Subsidy-war duration: if quick-commerce losses persist past FY2028, the margin-recovery slope that carries ~90% of the DCF value fails, and FCF stays negative into a third year.
  • AI capex overbuild: management targets ~10x its 2022 compute footprint and will spend "far beyond" RMB380bn/3yr — if MaaS monetization disappoints, depreciation permanently resets margins lower.
  • Regulatory/geopolitical: PRC platform rules, US chip export controls, ADR/WVR structure discounts — the 2021 antitrust fine (RMB18.2bn) is the template for how quickly the regime can reprice the stock.
  • Terminal-value reliance: 91% of DCF EV sits beyond FY2031 — the valuation is a claim about normalized economics, not near-term earnings.

Competitive Positioning↑

Alibaba is no longer China's traffic leader (Douyin) nor its price leader (PDD), but it remains the ecosystem with the deepest monetization toolkit, the only hyperscaler-grade cloud among e-commerce peers, and the broadest AI stack in China. Against Meituan it is the challenger (quick commerce); against Huawei/Tencent clouds it is the incumbent with 2x the share; against Amazon it is the same two-engine architecture at one-third the NTM P/E.

Entry Verdict↑

Attractive — stage the entry. Base intrinsic value HK$224.63 (bear HK$116.21, bull HK$285.43) against HK$94.10 makes the value case unambiguous, but the tape is in a confirmed downtrend (price 24.9% below a falling 100-day EMA, RSI 32) where oversold is stretch, not signal. Defer full commitment until price reclaims the ~HK$125 EMA100 with an upturning slope, or the HK$88.65 52-week low proves itself as support; the fundamental trigger to accelerate is the first quarter showing quick-commerce loss reduction with cloud growth ≥35% sustained.

1Company Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • High-quality core, deliberately obscured: the Taobao/Tmall advertising-and-commission franchise still earns a ~19% segment EBITA margin even while absorbing the quick-commerce war, and it funds every other bet the group makes.
  • The central debate is the FY2026 margin reset — RMB97bn of adjusted EBITA was consciously traded for quick-commerce share and the AI/Qwen build-out; the thesis turns on whether those losses normalize by FY2028 or prove structural.
  • Defining risk is concentration in one regulator's jurisdiction: platform rules, chip export controls and the WVR/Partnership structure all sit outside management's control, and the 2021 antitrust episode shows how fast the frame can move.

1.1Company Description↑

Alibaba Group operates China's largest e-commerce marketplaces (Taobao, Tmall) and China's #1 cloud/AI platform (Alibaba Cloud), monetizing merchants through advertising and commissions, consumers through direct sales and quick commerce, and enterprises through cloud computing and AI services.

Alibaba is a platform business: the core China e-commerce franchise connects ~62m+ high-spend 88VIP members and hundreds of millions of consumers with merchants who pay for traffic (customer management revenue — advertising and commissions), supplemented by first-party direct sales, a fast-growing quick-commerce network (Taobao Instant Commerce + Ele.me), international marketplaces (AliExpress, Lazada, Trendyol, Alibaba.com), and Cloud Intelligence Group — the group's second growth engine, now 15% of revenue and compounding at 34% on AI demand (Qwen LLM family, Model Studio MaaS, proprietary T-Head inference chips). The group is mid-pivot: FY2026 saw a deliberate, margin-dilutive investment cycle in quick commerce, the consumer Qwen app and AI infrastructure that management frames as building the next decade's platform.

LTM period used: FY2026 (fiscal year ended March 31, 2026). LTM revenue was RMB1,023.7bn (US$148.4bn, +3% YoY; +11% like-for-like ex Sun Art/Intime disposals), LTM adjusted EBITDA was RMB113.5bn, an 11.1% adjusted EBITDA margin (calculated: 113,483 / 1,023,670) — down from 20.3% in FY2025 on the investment cycle.

1.2Product Offering↑

Alibaba packages its offering as consumer platforms (free to use; monetized via merchants) and enterprise services (usage- and subscription-based): - Taobao — China's largest C2C/B2C shopping app; discovery-led marketplace. - Tmall — brand-flagship B2C mall for established brands; premium positioning. - Taobao Instant Commerce + Ele.me — sub-60-minute quick commerce for food and non-food categories. - AliExpress — cross-border retail export marketplace (Choice semi-managed model); Lazada — Southeast Asia marketplace; Trendyol — Türkiye/Gulf platform. - Alibaba.com — global B2B wholesale sourcing, plus the Accio AI sourcing agent and Accio Work agentic platform. - Alibaba Cloud — IaaS/PaaS, databases, security, and AI: Qwen model family, Model Studio MaaS platform, enterprise AI agents (Wukong, Meoo), and DingTalk collaboration. - Qwen app — consumer AI assistant, now integrating Taobao/Tmall shopping journeys. - Other consumer assets — Freshippo grocery, Amap mapping/local discovery, Alibaba Health, Fliggy travel, Hujing (digital media/entertainment), Cainiao logistics.

1.3Key Reporting Segments↑

Segment profit metric is adjusted EBITA (Alibaba's disclosed segment measure; segment EBITDA is not disclosed). Shares below are % of consolidated FY2026 revenue (RMB1,023.7bn); they do not sum to 100% due to inter-segment elimination (−RMB89.6bn) and unallocated items.

SegmentSegment DescriptionSegment Financials

Per-segment revenue and adjusted-EBITA trends are carried in the segment exhibit above; the series is FY2025–FY2026 only on the current reporting structure — the June-2025 reorganization breaks comparability with earlier fiscal years (FY2024 and prior are not restated).

1.4Geographies↑

Alibaba is a China-centric business with a measured international layer; the results announcements do not disclose a country-level revenue table (geographic revenue split: not disclosed in the dataroom's releases — the annual report's risk disclosures confirm the group derives the substantial majority of revenue from the PRC). - Using segments as a proxy (calculated), international commerce (AIDC) is ~14% of consolidated revenue; China e-commerce, most of Cloud and most of All others are domestic. - International footprint: AliExpress (global, strongest in Europe/Brazil), Lazada (Southeast Asia), Trendyol (Türkiye/Gulf; its local consumer/food-delivery arm was disposed in FY2026), Alibaba.com (global B2B), and a growing international data-center network for Alibaba Cloud. - Geographic risk is therefore predominantly single-country China exposure: consumption cyclicality, regulatory regime, and RMB reporting; AIDC adds EM currency volatility (revenue in local currencies, reported in RMB) and cross-border trade/tariff risk on the export-retail model. - Mix shift over time: AIDC has been the fastest-growing commerce segment (+9% FY26 on a +29% FY25 base), gradually raising non-China exposure, while the Sun Art/Intime disposals concentrated the domestic business back toward platform (asset-light) revenue.

1.5Revenue Model↑

Alibaba mixes high-margin recurring-like platform fees with lower-margin transactional volume: - Customer management revenue (CMR, RMB343.9bn, ~34% of group) — advertising (pay-for-performance, display) and commissions on Taobao/Tmall GMV. Effectively usage-based and repriceable; the new "business development program" ties merchant subsidies to marketing spend and is booked as contra-revenue (FY26 CMR +5% reported, +7% like-for-like). - Direct sales (China direct sales RMB105.5bn + most of All others) — first-party inventory (Tmall Supermarket/Global, Freshippo); gross-basis revenue, thin margins. - Quick commerce (RMB78.5bn) — delivery/commission revenue net of consumer subsidies (contra-revenue); currently loss-making, unit economics improving via order mix and AOV. - Cloud (RMB158.1bn) — consumption-based IaaS/PaaS plus Token Plans and MaaS subscriptions; enterprise contracts with committed spend; AI supply tightness currently supports pricing power. - Wholesale membership/value-added services (1688.com, Alibaba.com) — annual paying-member packages, the closest to contracted recurring revenue. - Revenue-model risks: CMR is tied to merchant ad budgets (macro-sensitive); take-rate is competitively constrained by PDD/Douyin; subsidy accounting (contra-revenue) can obscure gross momentum; no long-tenor contracted backlog is disclosed for cloud (not disclosed).

1.6Customers / Sales & Marketing / Distribution↑

  • Customer base: mass-market Chinese consumers (Taobao/Tmall), 62m+ 88VIP premium members (double-digit growth), global buyers (AliExpress ~180 markets), SME merchants and brands, and enterprises/developers on Alibaba Cloud. No customer concentration — no single customer is material (marketplace model); top-customer % not disclosed / not applicable.
  • Go-to-market: app ecosystems drive consumer traffic (Taobao app, Qwen app integration); merchant-facing direct sales and self-serve ad tools (Alimama); cloud sold direct and through a partner channel; Alibaba.com and 1688 sell paid memberships to SMEs.
  • Marketing intensity stepped up sharply: FY26 Q4 sales & marketing reached 21.9% of revenue (vs 15.3% a year earlier) on quick-commerce subsidies and Qwen app user acquisition — the single biggest controllable margin lever.

1.7Demand Drivers↑

  • China online consumption: national online retail sales grew +8.6% in 2025 to RMB15.97tn; physical-goods online sales +5.2%, 26.1% of total retail (NBS, accessed 2026-07-02) — a maturing mid-single-digit market that anchors CMR growth.
  • Quick commerce / instant retail: category sales grew ~28.6% in 2025 to ~RMB1.0tn (eMarketer, accessed 2026-07-02), with state media projecting >RMB2tn by 2030 (Xinhua, accessed 2026-07-02) — Alibaba's +47% FY26 quick-commerce growth rides this curve.
  • AI/cloud adoption: Mainland China cloud infrastructure spend rose 26% YoY in Q4 2025 to US$14.7bn, with 26% growth forecast for 2026 (Omdia, accessed 2026-07-02); Alibaba's AI product revenue has compounded triple-digit for 11 quarters.
  • Cross-border e-commerce: AliExpress/Choice rides EM e-commerce penetration; management flags FX and logistics costs as the constraints rather than demand.

1.8Cost Drivers↑

FY26 Q4 cost structure (% of revenue, GAAP): cost of revenue 65.5% (+3.9pp YoY), sales & marketing 21.9% (+6.6pp), product development 7.8% (+1.5pp), G&A 4.1% (−0.3pp). - Variable: traffic-acquisition and consumer subsidies (quick commerce, Qwen app) in S&M and contra-revenue; logistics/fulfillment and first-party COGS in cost of revenue; cloud depreciation grows with the AI build-out (adjusted EBITDA − adjusted EBITA gap widened to RMB37.1bn in FY26 from RMB29.3bn). - Semi-fixed: R&D/engineering headcount, data-center operations. Management notes server deployment cost has more than doubled in two years (chips/memory inflation) — capex-to-depreciation pressure will run for years (transcript, FY26 Q4 call). - Margin reality: what moves group margin near-term is the subsidy intensity in quick commerce + consumer AI, not the core CMR franchise, which remains highly profitable.

1.9AI Considerations↑

AI is the central bull-and-bear case simultaneously — Alibaba is China's clearest full-stack AI play, and the cost of that position is the current FCF inversion. - Opportunity (concrete): Qwen3.6-Plus LLM (1m-token context, state-of-the-art coding); Model Studio MaaS customer base up ~8x YoY; AI products ~30% of external cloud revenue with triple-digit growth; T-Head Zhenwu inference chips deployed >100k units on the public cloud with 30+ automakers — a domestic-chip moat under US export controls; enterprise agents (Wukong, Accio) and Qwen-app shopping integration re-anchor commerce demand into AI surfaces. - Risk (concrete): the build-out — management says required compute will "far exceed" the original RMB380bn three-year budget, targeting ~10x data-center footprint vs 2022 within five years — drove FY26 FCF to −RMB46.6bn; if MaaS/agent monetization or cloud pricing disappoints, the group has pre-spent a decade of capex. Consumer AI (Qwen app) is a cash-burning land-grab against ByteDance's Doubao with unproven monetization; open-source Qwen also arms competitors.

1.10Research & Development↑

  • Product development expense was RMB18.96bn in FY26 Q4 (7.8% of revenue, up from 6.3%); full-year R&D runs at roughly a high-single-digit % of revenue (calculated basis; FY26 full-year function table in the annual report).
  • Trend is decisively up — AI model training, chip design (T-Head) and cloud engineering — and Alibaba now spends at a scale comparable to global hyperscalers in RMB terms, far above Chinese e-commerce peers PDD/Meituan (peer figures to be tabulated at the valuation stage).

1.11Suppliers↑

  • Key inputs: AI accelerators and memory (global chip supply chain plus in-house T-Head designs; industry-wide tightness — management cites >100% two-year hardware cost inflation), data-center capacity/power, third-party logistics (beyond Cainiao), and content/traffic partnerships.
  • Supplier concentration: not disclosed. Structural risk is concentrated in advanced-chip access under US export controls — mitigated (not eliminated) by proprietary Zhenwu PPUs and heterogeneous-cluster orchestration software.

1.12Employees↑

  • 131,462 employees as of March 31, 2026 (up from 128,197 at December 31, 2025 — the first sequential increase after multi-year downsizing; headcount mix by function/segment not disclosed in the releases).
  • Unionization/CBA: not disclosed; PRC labor framework applies (no Western-style collective bargaining exposure disclosed). Watch item: gig-rider social-insurance rules for the quick-commerce fleet raise structural delivery costs industry-wide.

1.13Key IP or Facilities↑

  • Brands/platforms: Taobao, Tmall, 88VIP, AliExpress, 1688, Alibaba.com, Freshippo, Amap, Ele.me — among China's most valuable consumer marks.
  • Technology IP: Qwen LLM/multimodal family (HappyOyster world model, HappyHorse video generation), Apsara cloud OS, Model Studio, T-Head Zhenwu PPU chip designs, Cainiao logistics network tech.
  • Facilities: national and growing international data-center network (the ~10x expansion target vs 2022 is the group's defining capital project); Cainiao warehouses; Freshippo stores; Hangzhou campus HQ. Owned-vs-leased split not disclosed in the releases; PP&E, net stood at RMB282.7bn at FY26 year-end (+39% YoY), evidencing the build-out.

1.14Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑

Most-recent-first (last 5 fiscal years; consideration per releases where stated, otherwise not disclosed here): - FY2026 — Trendyol local consumer (food-delivery) business disposed; net disposal gains recognized. Buy-in of additional equity in non-wholly-owned subsidiaries (RMB14.7bn cash in Q4 FY26). - FY2025 — Sun Art (hypermarkets) and Intime (department stores) disposed — exit of asset-heavy offline retail; combined disposal losses recognized in FY25; revenue base reduced ~8pp (like-for-like adjustment). - FY2025 — Ele.me and Fliggy combined into Alibaba China E-commerce Group (June 2025 quarter; internal reorganization enabling the quick-commerce push). - FY2024 — Cainiao IPO withdrawn; Alibaba offered up to US$3.75bn to buy out minority Cainiao shareholders, re-consolidating logistics strategy. - FY2022–FY2023 — portfolio pruning era: progressive sell-downs of non-core listed equity stakes; the "1+6+N" holding-company restructuring announced March 2023 was subsequently rolled back into the current two-engine (e-commerce + AI/cloud) structure.

1.15Collateral Overview↑

Balances at March 31, 2026 (consolidated balance sheet, RMBm): - Working-capital assets: prepayments, receivables and other assets — current RMB251,837 (+25% YoY, partly quick-commerce/merchant financing related); no traditional trade-receivable concentration (consumer prepay model); merchant deposits liability trivial (RMB236). Inventory is embedded within other assets for direct-sales businesses (separate inventory line not disclosed in the release balance sheet). - Fixed assets: PP&E, net RMB282,699 (+39% YoY) — servers/data centers dominate additions; quality is high but technology-obsolescence-prone (AI hardware cycles). - Other balance-sheet collateral: cash + short-term investments + other liquid treasury RMB520,824; equity securities & other investments RMB479,996 (current + non-current); investment in equity-method investees RMB206,803 (Ant Group the largest); goodwill RMB247,378.

1.16Management Overview↑

  • Eddie Yongming Wu — CEO (since September 2023); also Chairman of Taobao & Tmall Group. Co-founder (1999) and Alibaba's first programmer/CTO-lineage executive; previously CTO of Taobao and Alipay, chairman of Wuyuan Capital (CNBC, accessed 2026-07-02). Architect of the AI-first strategy.
  • Joseph C. Tsai — Chairman (since September 2023). Co-founder; Alibaba CFO 1999–2013, then executive vice chairman; owner of the Brooklyn Nets (CNBC, accessed 2026-07-02). Deep capital-markets credibility; drove the capital-return program.
  • Toby Xu — CFO (since April 1, 2022). Joined from PwC in 2018 as deputy CFO; previously led Alibaba's China corporate finance.
  • Background check (third-party): no material personal litigation or fraud findings identified for Wu, Tsai or Xu in reviewed sources (CNBC, Variety, company announcements). Company-level regulatory history is material and disclosed under Other Risks: the record RMB18.2bn (US$2.75–2.8bn) SAMR anti-monopoly fine (April 2021) for forced exclusivity (Al Jazeera, accessed 2026-07-02) and the November 2020 halt of Ant Group's US$37bn IPO (CNBC, accessed 2026-07-02). Founder Jack Ma holds no executive role but remains a signalling figure for regulatory sentiment.

1.17Other Risks / Comments↑

  • Regulatory/political: PRC platform-economy regulation (the 2021–22 crackdown has eased but not disappeared); US-listing/ADR audit-access and delisting risk; US export controls on AI chips constraining the cloud roadmap; WVR structure via the Alibaba Partnership's director-nomination rights limits minority influence.
  • Investment-cycle execution: FY26 FCF was −RMB46.6bn and Q4 adjusted EBITA fell 84% — the model's earnings power is temporarily obscured; if quick-commerce discipline or AI monetization slips, the P&L reset could persist into FY2028.
  • Ant Group: 33%-owned equity-method stake; share of Ant profit fell to RMB375m in Q4 (from RMB1,763m) on Ant's own reinvestment; Ant remains structurally constrained from a re-IPO at former valuations.
  • Competition: PDD on price, Douyin on attention/live-commerce, Meituan/JD in the quick-commerce war (RMB10bn-scale subsidy commitments per player), ByteDance Doubao in consumer AI, Huawei/Tencent/ByteDance (Volcengine) in cloud.
  • Financial flexibility offset: RMB520.8bn liquid assets vs. ~RMB260bn total debt (bank borrowings + senior notes + converts + exchangeables) keeps the balance sheet comfortably net-cash even through the capex peak; FY26 shareholder returns were cut sharply (buyback US$1.0bn vs US$11.9bn in FY25; dividend US$2.5bn) to fund AI.

2Industry Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Alibaba is the only Chinese player positioned across every layer of the AI value chain — chip (T-Head), cloud (37% share of a market growing 26%), model (Qwen) and application — while its e-commerce home market matures to single-digit growth.
  • The industry's margin pool is migrating from marketplace advertising toward cloud/AI services and (eventually) instant retail; Alibaba is on the right side of the first shift and is paying heavily to not lose the second.
  • Quick commerce is a three-way subsidy war (Meituan/Alibaba/JD, ~RMB10bn commitments each) with regulators already discouraging "involution" — an oligopolistic truce is the base-case mechanism for margin repair, but its timing is unknowable.

2.1Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics↑

Alibaba straddles two distinct industries: (1) China online retail — marketplace e-commerce (advertising/commission-monetized platforms), first-party online retail, and the fast-growing instant retail / quick commerce sub-segment (sub-60-minute delivery of food and general merchandise); and (2) China cloud infrastructure & AI services (IaaS/PaaS, model-as-a-service, AI applications). Offline retail, logistics-as-a-service and fintech are adjacent but out of scope here. - E-commerce scale: China online retail sales reached RMB15.97tn in 2025, +8.6% YoY; physical-goods online sales of RMB13.09tn (+5.2%) equal 26.1% of total retail sales (NBS, accessed 2026-07-02). Third-party sizing puts the market at US$1.68tn (2026) growing ~9.5% CAGR to US$2.64tn by 2031 (Mordor Intelligence, accessed 2026-07-02). This is a mature, penetrated, secular-growth market — no longer a hyper-growth one.

  • Cloud scale: Mainland China cloud infrastructure spending hit US$14.7bn in Q4 2025 (+26% YoY), with 26% growth forecast for 2026 (Omdia/Canalys, accessed 2026-07-02) — re-accelerated from mid-teens by AI workloads.
  • Character: e-commerce is consolidated but contestable (attention-based entry via short video has repeatedly re-divided share), commoditizing at the transaction layer, differentiated at the traffic/ecosystem layer; cloud is scale-driven and consolidating, with AI capex raising barriers sharply. Both are heavily regulated (platform-economy, data-security and antitrust regimes).

2.2Structural Trends & End-Market Demand↑

Historical growth ran ~10% (online retail, 2021–2025 average per NBS releases); the forward mix shifts toward instant retail and AI services. - Core marketplace e-commerce (Alibaba's CMR base): secular, maturing. ~5–9% category growth (NBS; Mordor ~9.5% CAGR to 2031); demand drivers are disposable income and category migration online — increasingly cyclical with Chinese consumption sentiment. - Instant retail / quick commerce: the growth battleground. Category sales rose ~28.6% in 2025 to ~RMB1.0tn (eMarketer, accessed 2026-07-02); state-affiliated projections see >RMB2tn by 2030 (~15% CAGR) (Xinhua, accessed 2026-07-02). Driver is secular (convenience substitution) but current economics are subsidy-distorted: Alibaba and JD each earmarked ~RMB10bn of incentives against Meituan's incumbency (Momentum Works, accessed 2026-07-02).

  • AI/cloud: the structural accelerant. AI and agent adoption is the central driver of the 26% cloud-market growth (Omdia, above); demand is secular and supply-constrained — management across the industry cites chip/memory/data-center bottlenecks over a 3–5-year horizon, with >100% hardware cost inflation in two years (company transcript). Technology change (LLMs → agentic AI → AI-integrated commerce) is simultaneously re-platforming both industries; Douyin-style discovery commerce was the last such re-platforming and shows how fast share can move.
  • Cross-border retail export: AliExpress/Temu/Shein model growth is solid but tariff- and logistics-exposed (regulatory driver: de-minimis regimes tightening in the US/EU).

2.3Industry Structure & Value Chain↑

  • E-commerce structure: concentrated at the top — Alibaba, JD and PDD together control ~70% of GMV, with Douyin and Kuaishou compressing take-rates through social commerce (Mordor Intelligence, accessed 2026-07-02); analyst estimates put 2025 GMV at roughly Taobao ~$585bn + Tmall ~$568bn, Douyin ~$656bn (Business of Apps, accessed 2026-07-02 — platforms stopped official GMV disclosure in 2020–21, so all shares are estimates). Barriers: traffic ecosystems, merchant tooling, logistics density; weakest at the attention layer, where new entrants keep arriving.
  • Quick-commerce structure: top five platforms hold just over 60% of GMV — Meituan (largest on-demand fleet, 50k+ instant-retail warehouses targeting 100k by 2027), Alibaba (Taobao Instant Commerce + Ele.me), JD (Momentum Works; People's Daily, accessed 2026-07-02).

  • Cloud structure: clear CR3 — Alibaba Cloud 37%, Huawei Cloud 17%, Tencent Cloud 10% of Q4 2025 spend (Omdia, above); IDC's narrower public-cloud IaaS tracker shows Alibaba ~26.8% and rising. AI capex requirements (RMB100bn+/year for the leader) are a decisive entry barrier.

  • Value chain & margin pools: upstream chips/hardware (currently capturing outsized margin via scarcity) → cloud/compute platforms → models/agents → application platforms (commerce, work) → merchants/consumers. Alibaba is unique in China in occupying every layer from chip (T-Head) to application (Taobao/Qwen app). In e-commerce, the margin pool sits in advertising (CMR-type revenue); in quick commerce it is currently negative (subsidy war); in cloud it is shifting from raw IaaS toward AI/MaaS.

2.4Competitive Landscape & Positioning↑

  • Named competitors: PDD (value/price leadership, Temu abroad), Douyin/ByteDance (attention commerce; Doubao in consumer AI; Volcengine in cloud), JD (1P logistics-led retail, quick-commerce entrant), Meituan (quick-commerce incumbent), Kuaishou (live commerce), Huawei/Tencent/Baidu (cloud/AI), plus Shein (cross-border) and DeepSeek-style open-model labs (AI).
  • Alibaba's comparative position: #1 GMV ecosystem but no longer the traffic leader (Douyin); superior monetization toolkit and brand relationships (Tmall) vs PDD's price algorithm; the only Chinese player with hyperscaler-grade cloud + frontier open-source models + in-house inference silicon; quick commerce is a fast-scaled challenger (order share "fundamentally shifted" per management — company claim, not independently verified).
  • Market share: cloud share is well-evidenced (37% Omdia / ~27% IDC IaaS, above). E-commerce GMV share is estimate-only — roughly high-30s% of platform GMV across Taobao/Tmall on the Business of Apps figures, versus >50% five years ago; the share-loss trend to PDD/Douyin has decelerated but not reversed. Quick-commerce share: not reliably disclosed; Meituan remains #1.

2.5Margin Dynamics: Bargaining Power & Key Constraints↑

  • Merchants vs platforms: platform pricing power over merchants has weakened — merchant multihoming (PDD, Douyin) caps take-rate expansion, and the 2021 antitrust decision (RMB18.2bn fine for forced exclusivity — Al Jazeera, accessed 2026-07-02) legally bars the old exclusivity lever. CMR growth now has to come from ad-tech efficiency and subsidized merchant success programs, not raw fee increases.
  • Consumers/riders in quick commerce: consumers capture the subsidy war's value; rider social-insurance requirements add structural cost. Margin normalization depends on oligopolistic truce — plausible (three rational players) but not timetabled; regulators have already cautioned against "involution-style" subsidy competition (People's Daily, accessed 2026-07-02).
  • Compute suppliers vs cloud platforms: scarcity (chips, memory, power) currently sits upstream, doubling hardware costs — but it simultaneously hands pricing power to incumbent clouds with secured capacity; Alibaba management explicitly expects cloud gross margin to improve over 1–2 years as replacement costs reprice the market. The binding constraint on cloud growth is supply, not demand — a favorable structure for the #1 share holder.

3Financial Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Equity lens: FY2026 is an engineered trough — adjusted EBITDA fell 44% to RMB113bn and FCF went negative RMB47bn — while the base case rebuilds EBITDA to ~RMB340bn and EPS from RMB5.50 to RMB11.76 by FY2031E on subsidy normalization plus cloud scale.
  • Credit lens: even at the capex peak the balance sheet is net cash (RMB520.8bn liquid vs RMB260.0bn debt), interest cover is ~11.6x, and the negative-FCF phase is funded internally — solvency is not the risk; duration of the burn is.
  • Watch the reconciliation: reported net income (RMB102bn) is flattered by RMB74bn of investment gains; non-GAAP net income (RMB60.7bn, −62%) is the honest earnings line, and buyback resumption is the cleanest signal the trough has passed.

All figures RMB millions unless stated; US GAAP; fiscal year ends March 31. LTM = FY2026 (ended 2026-03-31). Adjusted EBITA / adjusted EBITDA / non-GAAP net income are company-reported measures; FY2027E–FY2031E are the CP3-approved base case.

3.1Financial Snapshot↑

Financial summary

3.3Financial Trend Assessment & Analysis↑

3.3.1Multi-year trends↑

Alibaba's five-year arc is a franchise transition financed by the core marketplace: revenue compounded at ~4.7% (FY2022–FY2026) while the profit base was deliberately reset twice — the FY2022 community-commerce investment cycle and the far larger FY2026 quick-commerce/AI cycle. FY2026 revenue of RMB1,023,670 (+3% reported, +11% like-for-like ex Sun Art/Intime) carried adjusted EBITDA of RMB113,483 — an 11.1% margin against 20.3% a year earlier and ~18.5% through FY2022–FY2025.

  • Revenue mix: Cloud rose from ~12% of consolidated revenue (FY2022, legacy basis) to 15.4% in FY2026 (+34%); quick commerce went from immaterial to RMB78,520 (7.7% of revenue) in two years; direct-sales revenue shrank with the Sun Art/Intime exits — platform mix is rising.
  • Margin bridge FY2025→FY2026: adjusted EBITA fell RMB96.6bn (−56%); the drivers are quick-commerce subsidies and Qwen-app user acquisition (S&M +RMB101bn to 23.9% of revenue) and technology investment, partly offset by CMR growth and AIDC's loss narrowing from RMB15.1bn to RMB2.1bn.
  • GAAP vs adjusted bridge (one-time): FY2026 GAAP operating income of RMB50,150 sits RMB26.3bn below adjusted EBITA on SBC (11.2bn), intangible amortization/impairment (5.1bn) and goodwill impairment (10.0bn, CEG-related); investment MtM gains of RMB87.5bn inflate GAAP net income — non-GAAP net income (RMB60,658, −62%) is the cleaner earnings read. Adjusted metrics are the analytical default hereafter.
  • FCF and balance sheet: analyst FCF (CFO + capex + intangibles) swung from +RMB129.7bn (FY2024) to −RMB50.7bn (FY2026) as capex tripled to RMB126.1bn and CFO halved on subsidy burn; the balance sheet absorbed it — RMB520.8bn cash & liquid investments vs RMB260.0bn total debt (net cash RMB260.8bn on the liquid-assets basis) after RMB22.3bn of convertible and RMB11.0bn of exchangeable-bond issuance.

3.3.2Most-recent interim↑

The March-2026 quarter is the investment cycle at full throttle: revenue +3% (+11% like-for-like), adjusted EBITA −84% to RMB5,102, and a GAAP operating loss of RMB848 — while the two growth engines accelerated (cloud +38%, external +40%; quick commerce +57%).

  • Cloud/AI: AI-related product revenue reached RMB8,971 in the quarter (~30% of external cloud revenue), the 11th straight quarter of triple-digit growth; management expects cloud gross margin to improve "meaningfully over the next one to two years" on replacement-cost-driven pricing power.
  • Quick commerce: unit economics and AOV improved sequentially on order-mix optimization; management describes a "fundamental shift in market share" versus March 2025.
  • Accounting change: the upgraded business-development program books select merchant subsidies as contra-revenue — CMR +1% reported vs +8% like-for-like; the optical CMR deceleration overstates the underlying slowdown.
  • Cash flow: quarterly CFO of RMB9,410 (−66%) and company-defined FCF of −RMB17,300 on capex of RMB26.9bn and Qwen-app acquisition spend; employees rose 3,265 QoQ to 131,462 — the first sustained headcount build since FY2022, concentrated in AI.

3.3.3Most-recent fiscal year↑

Management frames FY2026 as deliberate: like-for-like revenue +11%, with every incremental profit RMB from CMR (+5%, +7% LFL) and cloud EBITA (+35%) redeployed into quick commerce, user experience and the AI build-out. The structural read: the core franchise still generates ~RMB190bn of segment EBITA (CEG 107.5bn on a 19.4% margin despite absorbing QC losses + cloud 14.3bn), while "All others" carries a RMB35.7bn loss dominated by Qwen-app spend.

  • Segment economics: CEG margin fell from 38.0% to 19.4% — the entire group margin reset is inside CEG (quick commerce) and All others (consumer AI); AIDC approached breakeven, removing a RMB13bn drag.
  • Earnings quality: FY2026 net income of RMB102.1bn is flattered by RMB74.4bn of investment gains (MtM + Trendyol-local disposal); non-GAAP net income of RMB60.7bn (−62%) tracks the operating reality.
  • Cash: CFO −53% to RMB76.2bn; capex RMB126.1bn (12.3% of revenue vs 3.4% in FY2024); company FCF −RMB46.6bn — the first negative FCF year in the modeled history, funded from the liquid portfolio, modest net debt issuance and a US$1.0bn (vs US$11.9bn) buyback.
  • Credit view: even at the capex peak, net cash of RMB260.8bn (liquid-assets basis), interest coverage of ~11.6x on adjusted EBITDA, and a 3.8% effective funding cost leave credit metrics investment-grade-solid; the risk is duration of negative FCF, not solvency.

3.3.4Financial Forecasts↑

The base case buys the two-engine thesis but prices the transition: revenue compounds ~10% (FY2027E–FY2031E) on cloud 38%→20% and quick commerce 30%→8%, with adjusted EBITA margin recovering from 7.5% to 10.5% (FY2027E) and 16.0% (FY2031E) — deliberately below the FY2025 17.4% to respect the structurally lower-margin QC mix and cloud depreciation drag.

  • Earnings power: adjusted EBITDA roughly triples from the FY2026 trough to RMB339.8bn by FY2031E (20.6% margin); diluted EPS rebuilds from RMB5.50 to RMB11.76.
  • Cash: capex stays elevated (RMB155bn FY2027E, tapering to RMB95bn) per management's "far exceed RMB380bn/3yr" steer; analyst FCF stays negative in FY2027E (−RMB20.5bn), inflects positive FY2028E and reaches RMB190.5bn by FY2031E.
  • Capital allocation: dividends grow RMB20bn→29bn and buybacks rebuild RMB15bn→65bn as FCF recovers; leverage never exceeds 0.2x net debt/EBITDA at the FY2027E peak and returns to net cash by FY2029E.
  • Bull/Base/Bear (terminal FY2031E adj. EBITDA margin 18.0% / 16.0% / 11.5%): Bull needs cloud >40% sustained and QC profitability by FY2028; Bear is a persistent subsidy war plus AI overbuild — revenue CAGR ~5.5%, EBITDA recovery stalls near FY2025 levels, FCF stays near zero through FY2029E. The equity case hinges on the margin-recovery slope; the credit case is robust in all three.

3.3.5Guidance↑

Alibaba publishes no numeric revenue or EPS guidance; the outlook is qualitative and the model is calibrated to management's stated direction rather than a guided range.

  • Stated direction (FY26-Q4 call): cloud revenue growth to remain elevated with gross margin improving over 1–2 years; AI-product revenue sustained triple-digit; quick-commerce unit economics improving with scale; consumer-AI monetization progress expected within 1–2 years; capex to "far exceed" the original RMB380bn three-year plan (~10x compute footprint vs 2022 within five years).
  • Read-through: the base case's FY2027E cloud +38%, QC +30% with halved losses, and RMB155bn capex sit squarely inside that qualitative frame; the absence of numeric guidance widens the FY2027E outcome distribution and is itself a reason the scenario spread is wide.
  • Watch items for calibration: quarterly cloud external growth and AI revenue disclosure, QC order economics commentary, the FY2027 interim capex run-rate, and any resumption of the buyback at scale (a signal the board sees the FCF trough passing).

4Valuation & Entry↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Verdict Attractive with unusual asymmetry: the engine range is HK$116–285 vs HK$94.10 — even the bear case exceeds the price, and the reverse-DCF shows the market pricing a ~16% discount rate or perpetual ~10% FCF decline.
  • The mispricing driver is the FY2026 trough print plus a China risk discount: the market is capitalizing peak-investment economics and valuing the RMB974bn investment portfolio near zero; normalization of either closes part of the gap.
  • Entry stance: value Attractive, timing adverse — a confirmed downtrend (price 25% below a falling EMA100, RSI 32) argues for staged entry; the triggers are an EMA100 reclaim (~HK$125) or a held 52-week low (HK$88.65).

As of 2026-07-03. Price HK$94.10; comps in US$ (ratio columns FX-invariant); DCF engine in RMB, translated at CNY/HKD 1.1557.

4.1Overall Valuation Assessment↑

Alibaba screens Attractive on all three legs: the base-case DCF values the shares at HK$224.63 (+139% vs HK$94.10) with even the bear case above the price; the stock trades near its core-comp EV/EBITDA median despite carrying the #1 China cloud franchise the core comps lack; and the FCF-yield leg is the honest weakness — negative at the capex trough, recovering to a double-digit yield on the base case by FY2030E.

  • Intrinsic: base HK$224.63; bear–bull HK$116.21–285.43 (FCFF); FCFE conservative leg HK$141.25 — the entire engine range sits above the current price.
  • Relative: 13.5x EV/LTM adj. EBITDA vs core-comp median 12.6x and peer median 11.7x — a ~7–15% premium on a trough-EBITDA denominator; NTM P/E 10.2x vs peer median 12.0x (a 15% discount) and vs AMZN at 24.5x for the same two-engine model.
  • FCF yield vs cost of capital: LTM FCF yield −3.3% (capex peak) vs 10-yr CGB 1.9% and Ke 8.2% — the deficit is the price of the AI build-out; base case restores FCF yield above Ke by FY2029E.
  • Upside: cloud/AI compounding at supply-constrained pricing; quick-commerce truce normalizing ~RMB60bn of subsidies; buyback resumption on FCF inflection.
  • Downside: subsidy war persists past FY2028; AI capex overbuild (terminal value is 91% of EV); regulatory re-tightening or ADR/geopolitical discount widening.
  • The market-implied assumptions required to justify HK$94 are extreme (see Valuation Stress Test): a ~16% discount rate at g=3%, or perpetual FCFF decline of ~10% a year — both far outside any peer or historical anchor.

Valuation verdict: Attractive.

The valuation verdict is Attractive (base HK$224.63 vs HK$94.10), but the tape is in a confirmed downtrend with bearish momentum: oversold readings (RSI 32) are stretch, not signal. Defer full entry until price reclaims the falling 100-day EMA (~HK$125) with an upturning slope; the 52-week low at HK$88.65 is the line the downtrend must hold.

4.2Relative Valuation↑

CompanyPriceUS$Mkt capUS$MLTM RevUS$MLTM EBITDAUS$MEBITDA mgn%EV/EBITDAxNTM P/ExP/FCFxFCF yld%Div yld%

As of 2026-07-03. Multiples computed from the inputs (EV = mkt cap + net debt; FCF yield = FCF ÷ mkt cap), not lifted from a vendor. NTM P/E = price ÷ consensus forward EPS. Stats over peers only (subject excluded); Core-comp rows over the tier=core peers; n/m & n/a skipped. EBITDA basis — subject: company-reported Adjusted (step-4 model), fundamentals CNY→USD @0.1477, mktcap HKD→USD @0.1275; peers: reported (Yahoo), HKD→USD @0.1275 / CNY→USD @0.1477.

4.2.1Data caveats↑

  • Core comps (weight these): PDD, 9618.HK, 3690.HK — but note all three core comps' LTM EBITDA is depressed by the same quick-commerce subsidy war that depresses the subject's; the EV/EBITDA column is trough-on-trough (JD's 21.4x and Meituan's n/m are war artifacts, PDD's 3.7x reflects its huge net cash and zero payout).
  • Subject net debt uses balance-sheet cash + short-term investments (−US$4.0bn); a further ~RMB234bn of treasury investments sits in "equity securities and other investments" and is excluded here (conservative EV).
  • Net cash lowers EV for PDD, 9618.HK, 3690.HK, 0700.HK, 9888.HK; 3690.HK negative FCF/EBITDA → n/m; AMZN P/FCF distorted by its own capex cycle.
  • EBITDA basis: subject Adjusted (company measure) vs peers reported — modestly flatters the subject.
  • Sources: Yahoo Finance (AV daily budget exhausted; two-feed rule per methodology), accessed 2026-07-03.

4.3Intrinsic Valuation↑

The Gate-B-approved dual DCF (FCFF at WACC 7.48%, FCFE at Ke 8.18%; Gordon g=3.0%) values the operating business at RMB3,090bn EV, bridging to RMB3,739bn of equity — RMB194.36 / HK$224.63 per share base case.

  • Scenario range (engine-run, not derived): bear HK$116.21 · base HK$224.63 · bull HK$285.43; the FCFE leg's HK$141.25 is the conservative all-equity anchor.
  • Forecast drivers: revenue +10.8%→+8.6% (cloud 38%→20%, quick commerce 30%→8%); EBIT margin 9.0%→15.1%; capex RMB155bn→95bn; tax 20%.
  • Diagnostic 1 — terminal reliance: TV is 91.4% of EV (FY2026 is a deliberate FCF trough; unlevered FCF runs −35.4bn → +176.0bn across the window).
  • Diagnostic 2 — implied exit: the Gordon terminal implies 12.2x terminal adj. EBITDA vs ~16.3x current trading — the terminal is set below today's multiple.
  • Diagnostic 3 — reconciliation: FCFF−FCFE gap 37%, a discount-rate wedge (net-cash company; no true leverage benefit) — quote the FCFE leg as the floor.
  • Equity bridge: non-operating assets add RMB973.6bn (liquid investments 520.8 + strategic securities 246.0 + Ant/equity-method 206.8, both at book); NCI and mezzanine deducted. Investments-at-book is a judgment — the stress test carries the haircut case.

4.4Football Field↑

Triangulating the three methods against the market's own range shows the price sitting below every fundamental bar:

Method Low Mid High
DCF (FCFF) 116.21 224.63 285.43
EV/EBITDA Comps 27.14 81.49 147.84
NTM P/E Comps 59.53 110.66 226.11
52-Week Range 88.65 — 186.20
Current price 94.10

All bars HK$: DCF converted at CNY/HKD 1.1557; EV/EBITDA comps at USD/HKD 7.843; NTM P/E native. Peer bars apply reported-EBITDA multiples to the subject's Adjusted EBITDA (basis noted once).

  • The DCF and NTM P/E bars overlap in the HK$110–230 region; the price sits at the very bottom of its own 52-week range and below every method's midpoint.
  • The EV/EBITDA comps bar is the outlier low read — it applies trough-cycle peer multiples to the subject's trough EBITDA (double-counting the downcycle); it is the least informative bar this year.
  • Convergence read: methods agree the stock is worth materially more than HK$94; they disagree on how much, which is a terminal-assumptions question, not a franchise question.

4.5Valuation Stress Test↑

Both grids in HK$ per share (engine per-share × CNY/HKD 1.1557); shading low→high, base case ringed.

  • The verdict is insensitive: every cell of both grids sits above HK$94.10 — the minimum grid value (g=2.0%, WACC 8.48%) is HK$165, +76% vs price.
  • Reverse-DCF (analyst solve on engine outputs): holding g=3.0%, the discount rate that reproduces HK$94.10 is ≈16% — roughly double the built-up WACC; holding WACC at 7.48%, the implied perpetuity growth is ≈ −10% a year. Alternatively, zeroing the entire RMB973.6bn investment portfolio and NCI/mezz still leaves the operating DCF worth ≈ HK$185.
  • Terminal-value reliance (91.4% of EV) is the structural caveat: the valuation is a claim about FY2031+ economics, not about next year's earnings — appropriately stress-tested by the −10%-implied-growth finding above.

4.6Sources↑

  • Yahoo Finance quotes/fundamentals for peers and FX (accessed 2026-07-03); China 10Y CGB yield for Rf (per DCF input); engine artifacts _dcf/9988.HK_dcf_result.json, _dcf/9988.HK_dcf_cases.json.

4.7Technical Analysis↑

Technical read as of 2026-07-03. The setup is a confirmed downtrend with weak momentum: price sits 24.9% below a falling 100-day EMA at the bottom of its 52-week range, and oversold readings are stretch rather than signal.

  • Trend: price HK$94.10 vs EMA(100) ≈ HK$125.3 — 24.9% below, EMA slope −4.9% (falling); Bollinger %B 0.23 — lower half of the band, not pinned to the lower rail.
  • Momentum: RSI(20) at 32 — weak (30–50 zone), aligned with the downtrend; MACD histogram −0.57 — mixed/flat, no bullish cross.
  • Volatility: mid-band position after an extended decline; no compression signal that typically precedes reversal.
  • Volume: 66% of the 20-day average — light; the decline is not being confirmed by heavy distribution, but there is no accumulation footprint either.

Key levels:

Level Price (HK$) Basis
Support 1 88.65 52-week low — the level the downtrend must hold
Resistance 1 ~125.30 Falling 100-day EMA; reclaim with upturned slope = regime change
Resistance 2 186.20 52-week high

4.7.1Entry Assessment (combined)↑

The valuation verdict is Attractive (base HK$224.63 vs HK$94.10), but the tape is in a confirmed downtrend with bearish momentum: oversold readings (RSI 32) are stretch, not signal. Defer full entry until price reclaims the falling 100-day EMA (~HK$125) with an upturning slope; the 52-week low at HK$88.65 is the line the downtrend must hold.

Preferred entry zone: none while the downtrend regime holds — the trigger is an EMA100 reclaim (~HK$125) with positive slope, or a tested-and-held HK$88.65.

5Appendix↑ Contents

5.1Financials — Basis of Preparation↑

Item Value
Reporting currency / units RMB, millions (per-share in RMB; ADS = 8 ordinary shares)
GAAP framework US GAAP (HK-listed; audited consolidated statements in annual reports)
Fiscal year end / cadence March 31; quarterly results announcements
Historical window FY2022–FY2026 annual + 8 fiscal quarters (1Q25–4Q26); LTM = FY2026
Continuing-ops basis Single consolidated basis — Sun Art, Intime and Trendyol-local disposals were not classified as discontinued operations
Headline non-GAAP measures Company-reported adjusted EBITA (segment metric), adjusted EBITDA, non-GAAP net income; no analyst-derived substitution
Segment basis Post-June-2025 structure (China E-commerce, AIDC, Cloud Intelligence, All others); FY2025 quarters restated per FY2026 releases; FY2022–FY2024 carried on legacy 7-segment basis, flagged non-comparable
D&A convention Annual = CFS depreciation + amortization (incl. licensed copyrights); interim = disclosed depreciation + P&L intangible amortization (copyright amortization not disclosed quarterly)
FCF definitions Analyst FCF = CFO + CFS capex (incl. land-use rights) + intangible purchases; company FCF = CFO − capex (ex land/campus) − buyer-protection-fund changes; both carried
Interim CFS granularity Condensed (CFO/CFI/CFF/FX) + capex/FCF per company reconciliations; full working-capital itemisation is annual (quarterly detail not disclosed)
Projection base FY2027E–FY2031E built off FY2026 actuals; interest on opening debt; cash = CFS plug; circular-free

5.2Key Assumptions & Methodology↑

5.2.1Financial Model Assumptions↑

Assumption Basis Tag
CMR growth 6.0%→4.0% sourced: FY26 LFL +7% (release); contra-revenue drag fades sourced/assumed
Quick commerce growth 30%→8% sourced: FY26 +47%, UE improving (transcript); assumed deceleration assumed
Cloud growth 38%→20% sourced: Q4 exit +38-40%, supply-constrained demand (transcript); market +26% 2026F (Omdia) sourced/assumed
AIDC 10%→6%; All others −2%→+1%; elim +8%/yr assumed off FY26 actuals assumed
Adjusted EBITA margin 10.5% (FY27E) → 16.0% (FY31E) assumed: QC losses halve FY27 (mgmt UE commentary), cloud scale; capped below FY25's 17.4% assumed
SBC RMB12bn/yr; intangible amortization 4.5→2.5bn; depreciation 48→76bn sourced: FY26 levels + capex roll-forward sourced/assumed
Capex RMB155/150/130/110/95bn sourced: management 'far exceed RMB380bn/3yr', ~10x compute footprint target (transcript); assumed taper sourced/assumed
ΔWC −20→−10bn/yr assumed: merchant-financing receivable build moderates (FY24–26 −14 to −24bn) assumed
Tax 20%; interest 3.8% on opening debt (RMB260.0bn) sourced: FY25–26 effective rates sourced
Interest & investment income normalized +RMB12bn/yr; equity method +4→8bn assumed: strips FY26 MtM gains (+87.5bn); Ant reinvestment fades (transcript) assumed
Dividends RMB20→29bn; buybacks RMB15→65bn; shares 19,100→17,950mm sourced: FY26 US$2.5bn dividend; assumed buyback resumption post-capex peak assumed
Scenarios: Bull 18.0% / Bear 11.5% terminal adj. EBITA margin with cloud 45%/28% FY27E assumed: spans QC-truce timing × cloud durability debate assumed
DCF terminal block: WACC 8.0% (Rf 1.9% China 10Y, ERP 6.0%, beta 1.05, Kd 3.8%, D/V 13.6%), Gordon g 3.0% sourced components; g = min(China nominal GDP, terminal growth), implied exit EV/EBITDA cross-checked sourced/assumed

5.2.2Comps & Relative-Valuation Methodology↑

Item Detail
Peer sourcing Yahoo Finance only (Alpha Vantage daily budget exhausted; two-feed rule); accessed 2026-07-03
FX convention All peers converted to USD (CNY 0.1477, HKD 0.1275); subject fundamentals CNY->USD, market cap HKD->USD, hand-reconciled (per-field FX mismatch on HK tickers)
EBITDA basis Subject = company-reported Adjusted EBITDA (model LTM); peers = reported EBITDA (Yahoo) — basis difference flagged in table
Subject net debt BS cash + ST investments - total debt (-US$4.0bn); RMB234bn liquid treasury inside equity-securities lines excluded (conservative EV)
Trough-on-trough caveat All three core comps' LTM EBITDA depressed by the same subsidy war; EV/EBITDA column least informative this year
n/m handling 3690.HK negative EBITDA/FCF excluded from those stats; no peers excluded outright

5.3DCF Assumptions, WACC Build & Output Detail↑

Item Detail
WACC build Ke = 1.9% Rf (China 10Y CGB) + 1.05 beta x 5.5% ERP + 0.5% country = 8.18%; Kd 3.8% pre-tax / 3.04% after 20% tax; weights 86.4/13.6 -> WACC 7.48%
Terminal Gordon g=3.0% (Gate-B approved); implied exit 12.2x terminal adj. EBITDA vs ~16.3x market; TV = 91.4% of EV (flagged)
Forecast (base) FY27E-FY31E from S3 model arrays exactly: revenue +10.8%->+8.6%; EBIT margin 9.0%->15.1%; capex RMB155->95bn; tax 20%; UFCF -35.4bn -> +176.0bn
Scenarios (engine-run) Bear: cloud 28->12%, CMR +3%, terminal adj EBITA margin 11.5%, sticky capex -> HK$116.21. Bull: cloud 45->24%, margin 18.0% -> HK$285.43
Equity bridge EV 3,090,300 + liquid investments 520,824 + strategic securities at book 246,012 + equity-method (incl ~33% Ant) at book 206,803 - debt 259,996 - NCI 57,539 - mezzanine 7,845 = 3,738,559 RMBmm; 19,235mm diluted shares
Per share Base RMB194.36 = HK$224.63 @1.1557; bear HK$116.21; bull HK$285.43; FCFE conservative leg HK$141.25
Reconciliation FCFF-FCFE gap 37% — discount-rate wedge on a net-cash company with 91% terminal weight; FCFE leg quoted as floor
Reverse-DCF Price HK$94.10 implies ~16% discount rate at g=3%, or g ~ -10%/yr at WACC 7.48%; zeroing the full investment portfolio still leaves ~HK$185 (analyst solves on engine outputs)
Sensitivity WACC 6.48-8.48% x g 2.0-4.0%: HK$165-380; x exit 8.7-12.7x: HK$169-240 — every cell above the HK$94.10 price

5.4Peer Set Construction↑

Ticker Name Tier Rationale Notes
PDD PDD Holdings core Closest CMR/marketplace competitor No cloud; huge net cash compresses EV multiples
9618.HK JD.com core Scale China e-commerce + quick-commerce combatant 1P model; LTM EBITDA war-depressed
3690.HK Meituan core Quick-commerce incumbent — prices the war Negative LTM EBITDA/FCF (n/m in stats)
0700.HK Tencent read-across HK mega-cap platform/AI multiple anchor Games/social core
AMZN Amazon.com read-across Two-engine retail+cloud structural analogue US regime; premium multiple
9888.HK Baidu read-across China AI/cloud monetization read Declining ad core; smaller scale

5.5Data Gaps & Open Items↑

Item Why It Matters Where Flagged
GMV not disclosed since FY2021 Take-rate and market-share math rely on third-party estimates Company Overview §Key Reporting Segments
No country-level geographic revenue table International exposure proxied via AIDC segment only Company Overview §Geographies
Segment profit disclosed as adjusted EBITA only Peer margin comparisons must reconcile EBITA vs EBITDA bases Company Overview §Key Reporting Segments
Quick-commerce standalone profitability not broken out Sizes the central investment-cycle debate on the stock Company Overview §Key Reporting Segments
Segment history pre-FY2025 not restated to new structure Limits per-segment trend series to two fiscal years Company Overview chart spec 2
FY2027+ capex plan unquantified ("far exceed RMB380bn/3yr") Drives FCF trajectory and DCF capex assumptions Company Overview §AI Considerations
Disposal considerations (Sun Art, Intime, Trendyol local) not stated in releases M&A track-record pricing incomplete Company Overview §Key Acquisitions and Divestitures
E-commerce and quick-commerce market shares are estimates only Share-shift trend is directional, not precise Industry Overview §Competitive Landscape
Cloud share methodology spread (Omdia 37% vs IDC ~26.8%) Different market definitions; leadership claim robust, magnitude uncertain Industry Overview §Industry Structure
No third-party normalized quick-commerce margin benchmark Subsidy-war end-state economics unverifiable today Industry Overview §Margin Dynamics

6Disclosures↑ Contents

6.1Analyst certification↑

The views expressed in this report accurately reflect the analyst's personal views about the subject company and its securities. No part of the analyst's compensation was, is, or will be directly or indirectly related to the specific views expressed in this report.

6.2Rating definitions↑

This report uses a three-tier entry scale and publishes no price target: Attractive — intrinsic value materially exceeds the current price across the modeled scenario range; Fair Value — price sits within the modeled range of outcomes; Expensive — price exceeds intrinsic value under base and bull assumptions. Entry-timing comments (accumulate / starter buy / wait / avoid adding) are technical overlays, not ratings.

6.3Important disclosures↑

This document was produced for research-illustration purposes from public sources: company filings and results announcements (HKEX/IR), audited annual reports, earnings-call transcripts, and cited third-party sources (each with access date). Figures derived rather than reported are labelled as calculated. The financial model, DCF engine outputs and comparable-company data are available in the accompanying workbook and artifact files.

6.4Disclaimer↑

This material is not investment advice and does not constitute an offer or solicitation to buy or sell any security. Estimates and projections reflect assumptions that may not be realized; past performance does not guarantee future results. Investing in securities listed in emerging markets involves risks including regulatory change, currency fluctuation and market volatility. Recipients should conduct their own due diligence.

Sources & notes

Initiating-coverage research compiled from the company's filings, the data room and the analysis sections. Figures are as stated in the underlying sections. Not investment advice.
